heroCompanyDescription: Qantas Airways Limited is Australia's largest airline group and one of the world's longest-operating carriers, operating international and domestic passenger services, freight, and the Qantas Frequent Flyer loyalty program - one of the most valuable loyalty ecosystems in the Asia-Pacific region. The group operates through two primary brands: the full-service Qantas mainline carrier and the low-cost subsidiary Jetstar, which serves both domestic Australian and international leisure markets.

hero.embedded_thesis: At A$8.67 - near the lower end of its 52-week range of A$8.02 to A$12.12 - the market is pricing Qantas as a cyclically impaired airline facing a sustained fuel cost shock, despite a genuinely strong H1 FY26 result. The trailing P/E of 8.2x against reported trailing EPS of approximately A$1.06 reflects a distressed-cycle multiple, implying the market expects H2 FY26 and FY27 earnings to be materially lower than the H1 FY26 run-rate. The forward P/E of 6.9x against consensus forward EPS of A$1.1272 suggests the market is discounting even those already-reduced consensus estimates, pricing in further downward revisions driven by Brent Crude near US$100 and AUD/USD at US$0.69. The analyst consensus target of A$12.17 - a 40% premium to the current price - implies the market is applying a significant probability-weighted discount to the bull case, consistent with the current hypothesis distribution where downside scenarios (N3 plus N4) account for 55% of probability weight. The embedded thesis is essentially: elevated fuel costs will persist long enough to structurally impair H2 FY26 and FY27 earnings, and the A$105 million class action settlement and loyalty program changes add incremental headwinds.
hero.skew_description: The four hypotheses reflect a persistently bearish distribution, with downside scenarios (N3 at 42% and N4 at 13%) totalling 55% versus the recovery scenario (N1) at just 12%. The base case (N2) at 33% remains displaced by N3 as the modal scenario. The modest moderation in Brent Crude from US$108.79 to US$100.3 has not been sufficient to shift the dominant narrative, and the key swing factor remains the trajectory of oil prices through Q2 and Q3 2026.

next_decision_point.event: Qantas Trading Update / Fuel Hedging Disclosure and Q2 2026 Brent Crude Average
next_decision_point.metric: Average Brent Crude price through April-June 2026 and any Qantas management guidance revision, fuel cost hedging update, or H2 FY26 earnings commentary
next_decision_point.thresholds: If Brent Crude averages below US$90 per barrel through Q2 2026, the N2 base case reasserts and the stock likely recovers toward A$9.50+. If Brent Crude sustains above US$100 per barrel, N3 remains the dominant scenario and the stock risks testing A$7.00-7.50. Any Qantas trading update disclosing fuel hedging coverage above 60% of H2 FY26 requirements at pre-spike prices would be a significant positive catalyst. A formal earnings guidance downgrade from management would confirm N3 and likely push the stock toward A$7.00.

skew.rationale: The probability distribution is skewed bearish at initiation, with N3 (downside) and N4 (tail downside) together carrying 35% weight versus N1 (upside) at 25%. The primary driver of this skew is the extraordinary 42.8% surge in Brent Crude to US$102.44, which represents a severe and immediate headwind to airline unit economics that the H1 FY26 results -- reported before the full impact of this move -- do not yet reflect. The stock's proximity to its 52-week low of A$8.02 suggests the market is already pricing meaningful risk, but the fuel shock may not be fully discounted.

verdict.text: At A$8.67 - near the lower end of its 52-week range - Qantas is being priced as a cyclically impaired airline rather than the operationally strong business its H1 FY26 result demonstrated. The market is applying a 6.9x forward P/E against consensus EPS of A$1.1272, a multiple that implies those estimates are at risk of further downward revision given Brent Crude near US$100 and AUD/USD at US$0.69. The 40% gap between the current price and the analyst consensus target of A$12.17 reflects genuine uncertainty about whether the H1 FY26 earnings run-rate can be sustained, not a simple market mispricing. The confirmed A$105 million class action settlement and Jetstar lounge access changes add incremental headwinds but are not the primary drivers of the de-rating. The key question for the next 60-90 days is whether Brent Crude mean-reverts below US$90 - if it does, the stock has significant upside from current levels; if it does not, the N3 downside scenario of full-year FY26 PBT in the A$1.8-2.2 billion range will likely be confirmed by management guidance.

featuredRationale: The four hypotheses sum to 100%, with the base case (N2, 40%) carrying the highest weight as is appropriate for an initiation. The distribution is modestly bearish, with downside scenarios (N3 + N4) totalling 35% versus the upside scenario (N1) at 25%. The key swing factor is the trajectory of Brent Crude oil prices and whether Qantas's airfare pricing power can absorb the cost shock.

identity.overview: Qantas Airways Limited (ASX: QAN) — auto-added to coverage. Full company overview pending analyst research.

hypotheses.title: N1: Growth/Recovery
hypotheses.description: Qantas sustains its H1 FY26 earnings trajectory into the second half, with underlying PBT for full-year FY26 tracking toward A$2.6-2.8 billion. This scenario requires Brent Crude to retreat materially from current levels near US$100, loyalty program monetisation to accelerate, and airfare pricing power to offset residual cost pressures. At A$8.67, the stock would be deeply undervalued relative to intrinsic worth if this scenario materialises.
supporting: H1 FY26 underlying PBT of A$1,456 million, up 5% year-on-year, demonstrates sustained earnings momentum
Revenue rose to A$12.9 billion in H1 FY26, with trailing twelve-month revenue at A$24.6 billion
Management announced higher dividends and a larger fleet for FY26, signalling confidence in forward earnings
A new substantial holder emerged on 11 March 2026, suggesting institutional conviction in the stock
Basic EPS of A$0.45 for H1 2026 and trailing twelve-month EPS of A$1.06 imply a single-digit P/E at current prices
contradicting: Brent Crude at US$102.44 represents a severe fuel cost headwind that may not be fully offset by airfare increases
Stock is trading near its 52-week low of A$8.02, suggesting the market is not yet pricing a re-rating
A$105 million class action settlement and lounge access cuts signal ongoing reputational and cost pressures
No explicit broker consensus price targets available to anchor upside valuation

hypotheses.title: N2: Base Case
hypotheses.description: Qantas maintains solid top-line revenue growth and operational discipline from its strong H1 FY26 result, but Brent Crude near US$100 and AUD/USD at US$0.69 compress H2 FY26 margins relative to H1. Full-year FY26 underlying PBT likely lands in the A$2.2-2.5 billion range. The stock at A$8.67 trades at a discount to intrinsic value but the discount is justified by genuine earnings uncertainty through the remainder of FY26.
supporting: H1 FY26 results confirm the business is operationally sound with 5% PBT growth and revenue of A$12.9 billion
Airfare increases have already been implemented in response to higher jet fuel costs, partially offsetting the headwind
Dividend reinstatement and update on 11 March 2026 signals management confidence in cash generation
Stock is near its 52-week low of A$8.02, suggesting limited further downside if earnings hold
contradicting: Brent Crude at US$102.44 is a structural headwind that could erode H2 FY26 margins materially
A$105 million class action settlement is a one-off cash drain reducing free cash flow
Jetstar lounge access changes triggered a 2.75% share price fall, indicating market sensitivity to loyalty program erosion
AUD/USD at US$0.71 increases the AUD cost of USD-denominated fuel purchases

hypotheses.title: N3: Risk/Downside
hypotheses.description: Sustained Brent Crude near or above US$100 per barrel through H2 FY26 materially compresses airline margins, and airfare increases prove insufficient to fully offset the combined fuel and FX cost shock. The confirmed A$105 million flight credit class action settlement adds a one-off cash drain, while Jetstar lounge access restrictions risk loyalty program attrition. Full-year FY26 underlying PBT likely falls in the A$1.8-2.2 billion range, well below H1 FY26 run-rate extrapolations.
supporting: Brent Crude at US$102.44 represents a 42.8% surge, a historically severe headwind for airline unit economics
AUD/USD at US$0.71 amplifies the AUD cost of fuel, which is priced in USD
A$105 million class action settlement demonstrates ongoing legal and reputational risk
Jetstar lounge access cuts signal cost pressure and risk loyalty program degradation
Stock is already near its 52-week low of A$8.02, suggesting the market is pricing in meaningful risk
contradicting: H1 FY26 PBT of A$1,456 million demonstrates the business can absorb cost pressures at current levels
Management has already implemented airfare increases in response to fuel costs
Fleet expansion plans suggest management does not anticipate a severe demand downturn
A new substantial holder emerged in March 2026, indicating some institutional buyers see value at current levels

hypotheses.title: N4: Disruption/Catalyst
hypotheses.description: A tail scenario in which Brent Crude re-accelerates above US$110-120 per barrel driven by Middle East geopolitical escalation, or a domestic Australian recession materially reduces air travel demand. The combination of elevated oil, AUD/USD at US$0.69, confirmed legal liabilities, and deteriorating Australian construction and consumer sentiment creates a compounding risk environment. In this scenario, full-year FY26 underlying PBT could fall below A$1.5 billion and the stock risks testing A$6.00-6.50.
supporting: Middle East conflict is already cited as a driver of the 42.8% Brent Crude surge, with further escalation possible
Australian consumer confidence is under pressure from cost-of-living headwinds, which could reduce discretionary travel
Qantas has a history of reputational crises (ghost flights, COVID flight credits) that can trigger regulatory and consumer backlash
A$105 million class action settlement demonstrates the company is not immune to material legal liability
contradicting: Qantas operates significant fuel hedging programs that provide short-term insulation from spot price moves
The Australian domestic aviation market is a duopoly, limiting competitive disruption risk
H1 FY26 results demonstrate the business is generating substantial cash flow even in a challenging environment
Management's fleet expansion plans suggest no anticipation of a structural demand collapse

narrative.theNarrative: [Updated 02-Apr-26 08:38 AEST] Qantas Airways is trading at A$8.67 - near the lower end of its 52-week range of A$8.02 to A$12.12 - as Brent Crude remains elevated near US$100.3 and AUD/USD sits at US$0.69, sustaining a compounding fuel and foreign exchange cost shock that continues to weigh on the H2 FY26 earnings outlook. The stock has fallen 4.2% today, reflecting persistent negative market sentiment despite some moderation in oil prices from the prior peak of US$108.79. This deterioration comes against the backdrop of a genuinely strong H1 FY26 result delivered on 26 February 2026: underlying profit before tax of A$1,456 million, up 5% year-on-year, with revenue rising to A$12.9 billion and higher dividends announced. The A$105 million flight credit class action settlement was confirmed via ASX announcement on 12 March 2026, adding a one-off cash drain to the cost picture. Qantas has also announced a major overhaul to its lounge access policy effective 1 July 2026, restricting several frequent flyer benefits for Jetstar international passengers - a change that initially triggered a 2.75% share price drop and continues to weigh on sentiment around loyalty program value. The analyst consensus target of A$12.17 sits approximately 40% above the current price, but the market is applying a 6.9x forward P/E that implies consensus EPS estimates of A$1.1272 are themselves at risk of further downward revision if oil prices fail to retreat materially before the end of FY26.

priceImplication.label: Embedded Assumptions at A$8.67
priceImplication.content:
At A$8.67, the current price appears to embed the following assumptions:

1. Severe H2 FY26 earnings compression: The market is pricing a material step-down in H2 FY26 profitability relative to the H1 run-rate of A$1,456 million underlying PBT, driven by Brent Crude near US$100 and AUD/USD at US$0.69 - implying full-year FY26 PBT well below A$2.5 billion.

2. Distressed-cycle multiple: At 8.2x trailing EPS of approximately A$1.06 and 6.9x forward EPS of A$1.1272, the market is applying a multiple consistent with peak-cycle earnings risk, not a structurally improved airline business.

3. Consensus EPS at risk: The forward P/E of 6.9x implies the market believes even the already-reduced consensus EPS of A$1.1272 is too optimistic, and that actual FY26 EPS could print materially below A$1.00 if fuel costs remain elevated through H2.

4. Legal and loyalty headwinds priced in: The A$105 million class action settlement and Jetstar lounge access changes are being treated as incremental negatives on top of the fuel cost shock, rather than isolated one-off items.

5. 40% discount to analyst consensus: The A$12.17 analyst target implies the market is applying a substantial probability-weighted discount to the bull case, consistent with 55% weight on downside scenarios (N3 and N4).

narrative.evidenceCheck: The evidence base is consistent and reinforcing across multiple domains, with the weight of evidence supporting the bearish N3 scenario as the modal outcome. The strongest evidence comes from verified regulatory filings confirming H1 FY26 underlying PBT of A$1,456 million and trailing EPS of approximately A$1.06 - hard numbers that establish the earnings baseline. Against this, Brent Crude at US$100.3 - while moderated from the prior peak of US$108.79 - remains materially elevated relative to the levels assumed in pre-spike consensus models, and the AUD/USD at US$0.69 continues to amplify fuel cost headwinds in Australian dollar terms. The confirmed A$105 million class action settlement (12 March 2026) is a verified cash drain. The Jetstar lounge access policy change is confirmed and effective 1 July 2026. Analyst consensus EPS has been revised marginally lower to A$1.1272 for the current year, with the target price of A$12.17 remaining well above the current price - a gap that reflects analyst optimism about medium-term normalisation rather than near-term earnings support. The macro backdrop - Middle East geopolitical tension driving oil volatility, AIG Construction Index at -31.4, and broader Australian economic strain - provides corroborating evidence for the demand-side risk embedded in N3 and N4. The primary counterevidence to the bearish narrative is the strong H1 FY26 result itself, the 20% dividend lift, and the fleet expansion plans, which demonstrate operational discipline and management confidence. On balance, the evidence supports N3 as the modal scenario but does not yet confirm the more extreme N4 tail risk.
narrative.narrativeStability: The current market narrative - strong H1 FY26 earnings overwhelmed by a sustained fuel cost shock - is moderately unstable and sensitive to the trajectory of Brent Crude. The moderation from US$108.79 to US$100.3 has provided marginal relief but has not been sufficient to shift the dominant bearish thesis. The narrative now rests on three forward-looking tests: first, whether Brent Crude can sustain a retreat below US$90 per barrel through Q2 2026; second, whether Qantas issues a trading update or fuel hedging disclosure that provides visibility on H2 FY26 cost coverage; and third, whether the Jetstar lounge access policy change effective 1 July 2026 triggers measurable loyalty program attrition. A sustained oil price retreat below US$90 would be the single most powerful narrative-shifting event, likely driving a rapid re-rating toward the N2 base case and pushing the stock back toward A$9.50-10.00. Conversely, a re-acceleration of Brent Crude above US$110 driven by Middle East escalation would confirm N4 and risk testing the A$6.00-6.50 range. The confirmed class action settlement and lounge policy change are now known quantities and are unlikely to drive further material narrative shifts on their own.

evidence.intro: Evidence is assessed across ten epistemic domains, ranked by reliability. Each domain carries a distinct epistemic quality – from verified regulatory filings to unverified media commentary – ensuring the analyst understands what the evidence is before assessing what it means.

cards.title: 1. Corporate Communications
cards.finding: Qantas reported a strong underlying profit before tax of A$1,456 million for H1 FY26, a 5% increase from the previous year, with revenue rising to A$12.9 billion. The company also announced higher dividends and plans for a bigger fleet for FY26, reinforcing a bullish outlook. The A$105 million flight credit class action settlement was confirmed via an ASX announcement on March 12, 2026.
cards.tension: Despite positive financial results and fleet expansion plans, and the confirmed A$105 million flight credit class action settlement, the market reaction remains negative, with the share price falling 4.2% today to A$8.67. While Brent Crude prices have moderated slightly from previous highs to US$100.3, and the AUD/USD has slightly strengthened to US$0.69, these factors, alongside broader market pressures, continue to weigh on investor sentiment.
cards.source: Qantas Group HY26 Results ASX and Media Release (26 Feb 2026); Qantas Settles Flight Credit Class Action (12 Mar 2026); Qantas (QAN.AX) Falls 2.75% to 8.49 AUD After Jetstar Lounge Access Cut (swikblog.com)

cards.title: 2. Regulatory Filings & Financial Statements
cards.finding: For H1 FY26, Qantas reported an underlying profit before tax of A$1.46 billion, a 5% increase year-on-year, and revenue of A$12.9 billion. Basic EPS for H1 2026 was A$0.45, with trailing twelve-month revenue at A$24.6 billion and EPS at A$1.06. The company also confirmed a A$105 million settlement for a flight credit class action on March 12, 2026, and announced a dividend update on March 11, 2026.
cards.tension: While profit and revenue increased, the company continues to face inflationary pressures. However, Brent Crude prices have moderated to US$100.3 from the previous high of US$108.79, and the AUD/USD has slightly strengthened to US$0.69 from US$0.68. These shifts may offer some relief from previous extreme headwinds, though fuel costs remain elevated. Analyst consensus now estimates current year EPS at A$1.1272 (down from A$1.1343) and next year at A$1.2613 (up from A$1.26), with the PE ratio increasing to 8.1792 from 7.81, reflecting the recent share price decline.
cards.source: Qantas Group HY26 Results ASX and Media Release (26 Feb 2026); Qantas earnings: Profit up, higher dividends, and bigger fleet for FY26 (fool.com.au); Qantas Airways H1 2026 Margin And EPS Progress Reinforces Bullish... (simplywall.st)

cards.title: 3. Broker Research
cards.finding: Analyst consensus data indicates a Wall Street target price of A$12.17, with current year EPS estimated at A$1.1272 (previously A$1.1343) and next year at A$1.2613 (previously A$1.26). Key financial metrics include a PE ratio of 8.1792 (previously 7.81), a PEG ratio of 0.4031, a profit margin of 6.53%, an operating margin of 11.4%, and a dividend yield of 4.34%.
cards.tension: While specific broker reports are still not available, the updated analyst consensus data shows minor adjustments to EPS estimates and an increased PE ratio reflecting the recent share price decline. The target price remains significantly above the current share price, which continues to contrast with the recent market sell-off.
cards.source: Qantas Airways H1 2026 Margin And EPS Progress Reinforces Bullish... (simplywall.st); Qantas Airways Limited (ASX:QAN) - Shares, Dividends & News ... (intelligentinvestor.com.au); Qantas Airways Ltd, QAN:ASX summary - FT.com

cards.title: 4. Competitor Disclosures
cards.finding: The provided data does not include any specific disclosures or financial results from Qantas's direct competitors in the airline industry.
cards.tension: Without competitor disclosures, it is challenging to benchmark Qantas's operational performance, market share, pricing strategies, or financial health against its peers, making a relative valuation difficult.

cards.title: 5. Economic Data
cards.finding: Brent Crude oil prices have moderated to US$100.3 (from a previous high of US$108.79), though still showing a 29.0% increase today. The AUD/USD exchange rate has slightly strengthened to US$0.69 (from US$0.68), despite a 2.5% depreciation today. Qantas has already lifted airfares in response to higher jet fuel costs linked to Middle East conflict, and sector headlines continue to highlight soaring diesel prices impacting transport costs.
cards.tension: While Brent Crude prices have moderated from their previous peak and the AUD/USD has slightly strengthened, fuel costs remain elevated and the AUD is still depreciated. These factors continue to represent significant operational headwinds for airlines, potentially impacting future margins despite airfare increases, and remain a key contributor to negative market sentiment, albeit with some slight relief from the previous extreme levels.
cards.source: Provided Macro & Commodity Context (Brent Crude, AUD/USD); A Look At Qantas Airways (ASX:QAN) Valuation As Higher Fuel Costs Drive... (simplywall.st)

cards.title: 6. Alternative Data
cards.finding: Qantas announced a major overhaul to its lounge access policy, effective July 1, 2026, restricting several frequent flyer benefits for Jetstar international passengers. While this initially led to a 2.75% share price drop, the stock has since fallen further, with a 4.2% drop today to A$8.67, indicating broader market concerns beyond just policy changes.
cards.tension: Changes to customer loyalty programs and benefits could negatively impact customer satisfaction and brand perception, potentially affecting future bookings and loyalty, despite cost-saving intentions. This, combined with other negative factors like elevated fuel costs and legal settlements, continues to contribute to the negative market reaction.
cards.source: Qantas (QAN.AX) Falls 2.75% to 8.49 AUD After Jetstar Lounge Access Cut (swikblog.com)

cards.title: 7. Academic Research
cards.finding: No specific academic research, industry studies, or peer-reviewed articles related to the airline sector or Qantas's structural trends are provided in the available data.
cards.tension: The absence of academic research limits the ability to assess long-term industry trends, structural shifts, or the broader economic impact on the airline sector from a peer-reviewed perspective.

cards.title: 8. Media & Social
cards.finding: Recent media coverage highlights both positive financial results (H1 FY26 profit up, higher dividends) and negative events such as the confirmed A$105 million flight credit class action settlement (March 12, 2026) and changes in Jetstar lounge access. The market reaction remains negative, with the share price falling 4.2% today to A$8.67, reflecting increased investor concern over operational costs (fuel, FX), policy changes, and legal liabilities.
cards.tension: The increasingly mixed media sentiment, balancing strong financial performance against confirmed legal liabilities, customer-impacting policy changes, and significantly rising operational costs (fuel, FX), creates heightened uncertainty regarding public perception and future market reactions, contributing to the negative share price movement.
cards.source: Qantas shares flying through $105 million legal turbulence (fool.com.au); Qantas (QAN.AX) Falls 2.75% to 8.49 AUD After Jetstar Lounge Access Cut (swikblog.com); Qantas earnings: Profit up, higher dividends, and bigger fleet for FY26 (fool.com.au)

cards.title: 9. Leadership & Governance
cards.finding: Multiple "Change of Director's Interest Notice" announcements were made on March 6, 2026, indicating recent activity by company directors regarding their shareholdings.
cards.tension: The notices do not specify whether these changes represent director purchases or sales, making it difficult to infer management's confidence or concerns about the company's future prospects.
cards.source: Change of Director's Interest Notice (ASX, 6 Mar 2026)

cards.title: 10. Ownership & Capital Flows
cards.finding: Qantas announced an "Update - Dividend/Distribution" on March 11, 2026, indicating a return of capital to shareholders. Additionally, a "Becoming a substantial holder" announcement was made on the same date, signaling a significant change in the company's ownership structure.
cards.tension: While a dividend update is positive for shareholders, the "Becoming a substantial holder" announcement lacks details on the identity or strategic intent of the new substantial holder, which could have various implications.
cards.source: Update - Dividend/Distribution - QAN (ASX, 11 Mar 2026); Becoming a substantial holder (ASX, 11 Mar 2026)

evidence.alignmentSummary: Evidence across domains currently presents a mixed picture, with key discriminators remaining unresolved and significant data gaps in competitor analysis and alternative data. This lack of comprehensive information limits a definitive assessment of Qantas's near-term outlook.

discriminators.intro: The following data points carry high diagnosticity – they can meaningfully shift probability between competing hypotheses.

rows.evidence: H2 FY26 fuel cost per ASK (available seat kilometre) versus H1 FY26 -- the single most important metric to determine whether the Brent Crude surge at US$102.44 is materially compressing airline unit economics or being absorbed through hedging and pricing
rows.currentReading: Not yet reported -- H2 FY26 data will be available at the August 2026 full-year result. H1 FY26 showed 5% PBT growth despite cost pressures, but Brent Crude has surged further since the H1 reporting period.

rows.evidence: Qantas Frequent Flyer active member count and points redemption velocity -- the loyalty program is a key valuation support and the Jetstar lounge access changes (effective 1 July 2026) represent the first material test of whether cost-cutting is eroding the program's value proposition
rows.currentReading: The share price fell 2.75% to A$8.49 following the Jetstar lounge access cut announcement (effective 1 July 2026), indicating market concern over the program's value proposition. No post-announcement loyalty metrics are yet available to quantify member attrition.

rows.evidence: Identity and strategic intent of the new substantial holder (ASX announcement 11 March 2026) -- a strategic or activist investor acquiring a substantial stake could be a significant positive catalyst, while a passive index rebalancing would be neutral
rows.currentReading: Substantial holder announcement filed 11 March 2026 but identity and intent not disclosed in available data. This is a material information gap.

discriminators.nonDiscriminating:
• Revenue growth (A$12.9 billion H1 FY26): While impressive, top-line revenue growth does not discriminate between hypotheses because all scenarios assume continued revenue generation -- the key variable is margin, not revenue.
  • Fleet expansion plans: Management's commitment to a larger fleet for FY26 is consistent with both the bull case (capacity-driven earnings growth) and the bear case (higher capex and depreciation in a margin-compressed environment).
  • Dividend reinstatement: The dividend update on 11 March 2026 is positive but does not discriminate between N1 and N2, as both scenarios assume continued dividend payments at current earnings levels.
  • 52-week low proximity (A$8.02): The stock's proximity to its 52-week low is a technical observation that does not discriminate between hypotheses -- it is consistent with both a value opportunity (N1) and a stock in a downtrend (N3).
  • A$105 million class action settlement: This is a one-off item that is now resolved and does not discriminate between the ongoing earnings scenarios, though it does confirm the company's history of reputational risk.

tripwires.intro: The following catalysts could force a material revision to hypothesis weightings. Each carries conditional outcomes that map to specific narrative shifts.

cards.name: FY26 Full-Year Results

conditions.if: If full-year underlying PBT exceeds A$2.7 billion (implying H2 PBT above A$1.24 billion)
conditions.then: Then N1 bull case gains probability; stock likely re-rates above A$9.50 and potentially toward A$11.00 as market prices in earnings resilience

conditions.if: If full-year underlying PBT falls below A$2.3 billion (implying H2 PBT below A$0.84 billion, a material step-down from H1)
conditions.then: Then N3 bear case is confirmed; stock risks testing or breaking the 52-week low of A$8.02 and potentially falling toward A$7.00

cards.source: Qantas Group HY26 Results ASX and Media Release (26 Feb 2026); Continuum Intelligence analysis

cards.name: Jetstar Lounge Access Policy Change Effective

conditions.if: If Frequent Flyer member engagement metrics and forward booking data remain stable following the 1 July 2026 lounge access restriction
conditions.then: Then loyalty program risk is contained; the cost-saving rationale is validated and the market narrative stabilises around N2 base case

conditions.if: If media reports or management commentary indicate accelerated Frequent Flyer member attrition or booking softness following the policy change
conditions.then: Then loyalty program erosion risk escalates toward N3; the Qantas brand premium is structurally impaired and the stock faces further de-rating

cards.source: Qantas (QAN.AX) Falls 2.75% to 8.49 AUD After Jetstar Lounge Access Cut (swikblog.com); Qantas Settles Flight Credit Class Action (ASX, 12 Mar 2026)

cards.name: Brent Crude Price Trajectory (Q2 2026 Average)

conditions.if: If Brent Crude averages below US$90 per barrel through April-June 2026, driven by geopolitical de-escalation or demand destruction
conditions.then: Then H2 FY26 fuel cost headwind is materially reduced; N1 bull case probability increases and the stock could recover toward A$10.00

conditions.if: If Brent Crude remains above US$100 per barrel or escalates further toward US$120 per barrel through Q2 2026
conditions.then: Then N3/N4 downside scenarios gain probability; H2 FY26 margin compression is locked in and the stock risks breaking below A$8.00

cards.source: Provided Macro and Commodity Context (Brent Crude US$102.44, +42.8%); A Look At Qantas Airways (ASX:QAN) Valuation As Higher Fuel Costs Drive... (simplywall.st)

cards.name: Qantas Fuel Hedging / Trading Update (Q2 2026)

conditions.if: If Qantas issues a trading update disclosing fuel hedging coverage above 60% of H2 FY26 requirements at pre-spike prices, or if Brent Crude retreats below US$90 per barrel by end of April 2026
conditions.then: Then H2 FY26 margin compression is materially reduced, N2 base case reasserts, and the stock likely re-rates toward A$9.50-A$10.50 as the distressed-cycle multiple unwinds

conditions.if: If Brent Crude sustains above US$105 per barrel through April 2026 with no Qantas hedging disclosure, or if management issues a profit warning for H2 FY26
conditions.then: Then N3 becomes the dominant scenario, full-year FY26 PBT risks falling below A$2.2 billion, and the stock tests the A$7.00-A$7.50 range with potential for further downside toward A$6.50

cards.source: Brent Crude futures market data; Qantas ASX announcements portal; H1 FY26 results fuel hedging disclosures (26 Feb 2026)

cards.name: Qantas H2 FY26 Trading Update / Earnings Guidance Revision

conditions.if: If Qantas issues a trading update confirming H2 FY26 underlying PBT tracking above A$1.1 billion, supported by evidence of fuel hedging coverage or Brent Crude mean-reversion below US$90
conditions.then: Then N2 base case reasserts as modal scenario, stock likely recovers toward A$9.50-10.50, and analyst consensus estimates are validated

conditions.if: If Qantas issues a formal earnings guidance downgrade for FY26, citing sustained Brent Crude above US$105 and AUD/USD weakness, with H2 FY26 PBT tracking below A$800 million
conditions.then: Then N3 becomes the confirmed dominant scenario, stock risks testing A$6.50-7.00, and analyst consensus target of A$12.17 faces material downward revision

cards.source: ASX announcement / Qantas Investor Centre; informed by H1 FY26 results commentary and current macro trajectory

cards.name: Jetstar Lounge Access Policy Change Takes Effect

conditions.if: If Frequent Flyer member engagement metrics and forward booking data remain stable following the 1 July 2026 lounge access restriction for Jetstar international passengers
conditions.then: Then loyalty program resilience is confirmed, reducing the risk of revenue attrition and supporting the N2 base case; the policy change is absorbed as a cost-saving measure without material brand damage

conditions.if: If forward booking data or Frequent Flyer engagement metrics show measurable deterioration following the 1 July 2026 lounge access restriction
conditions.then: Then loyalty program attrition risk escalates, adding a revenue headwind on top of the existing fuel cost pressure and reinforcing the N3 downside scenario; management may be forced to partially reverse the policy

cards.source: Qantas ASX announcements and media coverage of lounge access policy overhaul, confirmed effective 1 July 2026

coverageRows.freshness: 12 Mar 2026 (Class Action Settlement); 11 Mar 2026 (Dividend Update, Substantial Holder)
coverageRows.confidence: High -- ASX-lodged results and media releases are current and detailed
High -- verified financial statements with specific PBT, revenue, and EPS figures
Medium -- specific EPS estimates and price targets are available, but detailed reports or ratings are not.
Low -- no Virgin Australia, Rex, or international peer data available for benchmarking
High for commodity prices; Medium for Australian consumer demand and travel outlook
Medium -- lounge access change is documented but no booking data, load factors, or yield data available

trend.structure: Price in downtrend relative to 50-day and 200-day moving averages.

footer.disclaimer: This report does not constitute personal financial advice. Continuum Intelligence synthesises cross-domain evidence using the Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH) methodology. All factual claims are sourced from ASX filings, company disclosures, broker consensus data, and publicly available information.

priceDrivers.primary_driver: A probable company-specific negative catalyst around 26 February 2026, most likely the Qantas H1 FY2026 interim results or an associated guidance revision, triggered an initial 9% single-session decline that was then compounded by a broad macro risk-off environment and sector-wide selling through March 2026; however, this primary attribution rests on price-action inference alone, as no ASX filing, news article, or broker note is present in the dataset to confirm it.

primary: Probable company-specific earnings or guidance event circa 25-26 February 2026, inferred from a 9.2% single-session drop on 26 February with no subsequent recovery (unconfirmed: no ASX announcement, dated news article, or broker note available to verify)
secondary: Broad macro risk-off and global trade or tariff shock compressing airline valuations and demand expectations through March 2026, consistent with the ASX 200 falling approximately 7.7% from 1 to 29 March 2026
Sector-wide pressure via the ASX Industrials ETF (^AXNJ) declining approximately 8.0% across the same March window
amplifiers: Technical breakdown below key support at A$9.00 and A$8.82 in early March 2026, likely triggering stop-loss and systematic selling

Probable but unconfirmed broker target price revisions following any earnings event, consistent with the unusually large gap between the consensus mean target of A$12.13 and the current price of A$8.67
Possible short-covering and technical support buying near the 52-week low of A$8.02, contributing to the 10-day relative outperformance of 2.49% versus the ASX 200 into 1 April 2026
background: QAN had already retraced from its August 2025 peak of A$12.12 to approximately A$10.65 by late February 2026, leaving the stock technically extended and vulnerable to an earnings disappointment
Airline operating leverage creates inherent beta to both macro demand shocks and input cost moves; jet fuel price data is absent from the dataset but remains a material background variable

Total debt of A$8.2 billion against EBITDA of A$4.35 billion (gross leverage approximately 1.9x) provides limited cushion if forward earnings disappoint, heightening investor sensitivity to guidance changes

Forward PE of 6.88x and EV/EBITDA of 4.49x are low in absolute terms but structurally consistent with airline sector multiples; they do not confirm cheap valuation without confidence in forward earnings
rejected: Sentiment or consumer reputational backlash as a primary driver: no HotCopper, Reddit, or media data available; even if present, reputation events alone do not typically produce a 19% sustained decline in a large-cap stock
Index rebalancing or passive fund flows as a primary driver: speculative with no flow data; may be a minor amplifier near the 52-week low but does not explain the step-change on 26 February

The headline daily change of -4.2% on the analysis date as a discrete driver: this figure appears inconsistent with the price history, which records identical closes of A$8.67 on both 31 March and 1 April 2026; it likely reflects an intraday move that recovered or a data alignment artefact and should not be treated as a confirmed session decline

report.title: QAN: 19% Decline From February Peak Driven by Probable Earnings Shock and Macro Headwinds, Primary Catalyst Unconfirmed
report.executive_summary: Qantas shares fell approximately 19% from A$10.65 on 24 February 2026 to a low of A$8.15 by 22 March 2026, with the sharpest single-session move occurring on 25-26 February when the stock dropped roughly 9.2% in one day. The most plausible explanation for that initial dislocation is a company-specific negative catalyst, most likely the Qantas H1 FY2026 interim result or an associated guidance revision, consistent with the airline's typical February reporting calendar. This attribution is, however, an inference from price action alone: no ASX announcement, dated news article, or broker note exists in the available dataset to confirm it. The subsequent decline through March was compounded by a broad macro risk-off move that took the ASX 200 down approximately 7.7% and the Industrials sector ETF down approximately 8.0% over the same period. QAN underperformed the index by roughly 4.4 percentage points on a cumulative basis through March, consistent with either an earnings overhang or operating leverage amplifying a macro shock. Near the 52-week low, the stock has modestly stabilised, with 10-day relative outperformance of 2.49% into 1 April 2026.
report.full_note:
Qantas fell approximately 19% from its 24 February 2026 close of A$10.65 to an intraday low near A$8.15 by 22 March, before partially recovering to A$8.67 by 1 April. The most likely primary cause of the initial dislocation is a company-specific negative earnings or guidance event on or around 25-26 February 2026, which is consistent with Qantas's typical half-year results timing. A single-session decline of approximately 9.2% of this kind, with no subsequent recovery in the following sessions, is the hallmark of a fundamental re-rating rather than a technical or flow event. It is important to state clearly, however, that this conclusion is an inference drawn entirely from price action: the dataset contains no ASX announcement filings, no dated news articles referencing the session, and no broker notes for the period. The primary driver must therefore be labelled as probable but unconfirmed.

The strongest supporting evidence is the price action itself. The 26 February session is the single largest discrete move in the entire observable window and stands apart from the broader market decline that followed in March. The absence of any comparable single-day dislocation in the ASX 200 index data for early March suggests the initial event was not purely market-wide, though a critical evidence gap exists: ASX 200 data for 25-26 February itself is not available in the dataset, meaning the move cannot yet be formally isolated as stock-specific. Qantas's half-year results are typically released in mid-to-late February; a result that missed earnings expectations or included a forward guidance downgrade would explain the magnitude, the timing, and the absence of any price recovery in subsequent sessions.

The subsequent decline through March was materially amplified by a broad macro deterioration. The ASX 200 fell approximately 7.7% from 1 to 29 March, and the ASX Industrials sector ETF declined approximately 8.0% over the same period. Airlines are structurally high-beta to macro risk-off episodes: operating leverage, fuel cost sensitivity, and the discretionary nature of leisure travel all reprice simultaneously when growth expectations deteriorate. QAN's approximately 12.1% decline from 1 to 29 March against the index's 7.7% move implies roughly 4.4 percentage points of cumulative underperformance, which is consistent with either a residual company-specific earnings overhang or with airlines simply absorbing more of the macro shock due to their cost structure. Jet fuel price data is absent from the dataset but would be a critical additional variable; any sustained move in Brent crude during this period would have compounded the pressure on airline margins.

From a volume and technical perspective, the breach of A$9.00 on 3 March and A$8.82 shortly after likely triggered systematic and stop-loss selling, extending the move beyond what fundamental repricing alone would justify. The 52-week low of A$8.02, set in April 2025, appears to have acted as meaningful support in mid-to-late March, and the 10-day relative outperformance of 2.49% versus the ASX 200 into 1 April suggests some combination of technical support buying and possible short covering near that level. Elevated volume on 1 April, at approximately 11.4 million shares, is consistent with institutional repositioning near the lows but cannot be attributed to a confirmed catalyst. The flow interpretation remains speculative.

On social and sentiment channels, no HotCopper, Reddit, or media data is available in the dataset. For a large-cap stock of this type, social activity would be expected to lag or amplify a fundamental move rather than lead it, and this assessment is unchanged in the absence of data. Social channels are treated as irrelevant to this analysis.

Several alternative explanations were examined and rejected as primary drivers. A consumer reputational backlash, while consistent with Qantas's history of periodic public criticism, does not produce 19% sustained declines in large-cap airline stocks without an accompanying financial disclosure. Index rebalancing and passive flow mechanics may have contributed marginally near the 52-week low but cannot explain the step-change event on 26 February. The headline daily change of -4.2% shown in the snapshot data for 1 April is inconsistent with the price history, which records identical closes on 31 March and 1 April at A$8.67; this figure appears to be a data artefact and has not been treated as a driver.

The most significant analytical concern is the gap between the analyst consensus mean target of A$12.13 and the current price of A$8.67, a differential of approximately 40%. This is either a sign that sell-side targets have not been updated since the decline, which would make a wave of broker downgrades both likely and currently unpriced, or it reflects the market discounting a deeper earnings deterioration than consensus models have incorporated. Both scenarios are bearish on a forward basis. The view that a company-specific earnings event drove the initial decline would be confirmed by the release of ASX filing data for February-March 2026, dated media coverage of the 26 February session, and broker notes issued in the days following that date. If those sources reveal instead that the broader market also sold off sharply on 25-26 February, the macro and sector-wide explanation would be elevated to co-primary status and the confidence assessment would need to be revised accordingly.

evidence_quality.primary_evidence: Price history confirms a 9.2% single-session decline on approximately 25-26 February 2026, a sustained multi-week downtrend from A$10.65 to A$8.15, and a partial recovery to A$8.67 by 1 April. ASX 200 index data from 1 March confirms a broad market decline of approximately 7.7% over March. The ASX Industrials sector ETF confirms approximately 8.0% sector-level weakness over the same period. These are data-quality confirmed items.
evidence_quality.secondary_evidence: The analyst consensus mean target of A$12.13 versus the current price of A$8.67 implies either stale consensus or a market-implied earnings deterioration not yet reflected in sell-side models. Forward PE of 6.88x and EV/EBITDA of 4.49x suggest the market is pricing significant earnings risk. Both are confirmed financial data points, though their interpretation is inferential.
evidence_quality.key_gap: The single most critical gap is the absence of ASX announcement filings, dated news articles, and broker notes for the period from 1 February to 1 April 2026. Without these, the specific catalyst for the 26 February single-session decline cannot be confirmed. Equally important is the absence of ASX 200 index data for 25-26 February, which means the move cannot yet be formally isolated as stock-specific versus market-wide. Jet fuel and crude oil price data for the February to April 2026 window is also absent and would materially inform the earnings and margin outlook for QAN.

what_would_change_the_view: ASX announcement filings confirming a Qantas H1 FY2026 results release on or around 25-26 February 2026 with a material earnings miss or guidance downgrade would elevate confidence in the primary driver from probable to confirmed and upgrade overall confidence from low to high

ASX 200 index data for 25-26 February 2026 showing a broad market decline of comparable magnitude on those exact dates would shift the primary attribution from company-specific to macro or sector-wide, demoting the earnings hypothesis to secondary status

Broker notes from late February or early March 2026 showing consensus target price cuts to the A$8-10 range would confirm that the sustained multi-week selling reflected a coordinated analyst re-rating rather than purely price-action-driven selling

Evidence that the ASX Industrials sector ETF also fell sharply on 25-26 February, rather than only from 1 March onwards, would suggest the entire decline is macro and sector-driven and that the company-specific hypothesis is overstated

broker_activity.consensus_change: No broker upgrade, downgrade, or note data is present in the dataset. The gap between the consensus mean target of A$12.13 and the current price of A$8.67 is approximately 40%, which is either a sign of severely stale consensus targets or reflects a market pricing in earnings deterioration that sell-side models have not yet captured. This is the most critical unresolved evidence gap in the analysis.
